questions.question: A YOLO detector with grid 13x13, 3 anchors per cell, and 20 classes produces a head output of shape (N, 13, 13, 3, 25). Why is the last dimension 25?
options: 4 box coords + 1 bias + 20 classes
4 box coords + 1 objectness + 20 classes = 5 + C
25 is the number of classes in COCO
It is padding to the next power of 2
questions.explanation: Every anchor emits four box regression targets (tx, ty, tw, th), one objectness score, and C class logits. For YOLO-style detectors the per-anchor dimension is always 5 + C. On COCO with 80 classes it is 85; on PASCAL VOC with 20 it is 25.

questions.question: In the YOLO decode step, `width = anchor_w * exp(tw)`. An anchor has width 75 px and the network outputs tw = 0.693. Approximately what decoded width results?
options: 37.5 px — exp(0.693) halves the anchor
75 px — exp(0.693) ≈ 1 so no change
150 px — exp(0.693) ≈ 2 so the width doubles
693 px — tw is multiplied directly
questions.explanation: exp(0.693) ≈ 2, so width = 75 × 2 = 150 px. Using exp ensures width is always positive and can grow or shrink relative to the anchor without a sign constraint. tw = 0 recovers the anchor width exactly; negative tw shrinks it below the anchor, positive tw grows it above.

questions.question: In `yolo_loss`, `lambda_noobj` is set to 0.5 by default. What symptom would you expect if you raised it to match `lambda_obj = 1.0`?
options: The hundreds of empty cells would produce overwhelming objectness loss that suppresses the positive signal, teaching the model to predict zero objectness everywhere and collapsing recall
Training would converge faster because more cells contribute signal
Box regression loss would decrease as empty cells are upweighted
NMS would suppress more boxes at inference time
questions.explanation: A typical 13×13 grid has 169 cells, most containing no object. At lambda_noobj = lambda_obj each empty cell contributes as much to the total loss as an occupied one. Since empty cells vastly outnumber occupied cells, their combined loss dominates and the network learns to output near-zero objectness everywhere — recall collapses.

questions.question: The `assign_targets` function picks the best anchor by computing IoU between the ground-truth box shape and each anchor shape, ignoring position. Why shape IoU rather than position IoU?
options: Position IoU requires running the full decode step first
Shape IoU is a requirement of the PyTorch autograd engine
Position IoU always evaluates to zero before predictions are decoded

Shape IoU picks the anchor prior whose aspect ratio best matches the ground truth; the position offset is handled separately by the regression targets tx and ty, so it should not influence which prior is selected
questions.explanation: At assignment time the question is 'which anchor shape fits this object's dimensions best?' — not where in the cell the object is. Shape IoU matches the YOLOv2/v3 design: the chosen anchor provides the starting geometry and the network regresses (tx, ty, tw, th) deltas on top of it. Mixing position into anchor selection would make the assignment dependent on the current prediction quality.

questions.question: Your model has mAP@0.5 = 0.75 but mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.32. What is the most accurate interpretation?
options: The model is broken; mAP@0.5 must equal mAP@0.5:0.95
The model finds the right objects but its boxes are not tightly localised — fine at IoU 0.5 but failing at IoU 0.7+
The dataset has too many classes for the anchor set
The inference confidence threshold is too high
questions.explanation: mAP@0.5 is lenient about localisation; mAP@0.5:0.95 averages over strict thresholds up to 0.95. A large gap means the boxes are in roughly the right place but not tight. Typical fixes: CIoU/DIoU box loss (which optimises IoU directly instead of MSE), higher-resolution feature maps, or an anchor set better tuned to the dataset's box shapes.

questions.question: YOLO loss uses lambda_coord = 5.0 and lambda_noobj = 0.5. What engineering logic explains both values simultaneously?
options: GPU memory layout determines the optimal lambda ratios
They are random choices from the original paper with no theoretical basis
The number of anchor boxes per cell sets the lambda scale

Most cells are empty so the no-object signal must be downweighted or it drowns the positive examples; box regression (MSE) is numerically small compared to cross-entropy losses so it must be upweighted to produce comparable gradient magnitudes
questions.explanation: lambda_noobj < 1 tames the hundreds of empty cells that would otherwise overwhelm the handful containing real objects. lambda_coord > 1 compensates for the fact that MSE loss on small (tx, ty, tw, th) values is numerically much smaller than BCE or cross-entropy, so without upweighting the box head's gradients vanish next to the classification signals. Both ratios balance per-component gradient magnitudes.
